definition: Feldenkrais Method framework for movement awareness. Seven dimensions: Sequence (chain of motion through skeleton), Path (line of movement in space), Initiation (where movement begins), Foundation (relationship of weight to support), Flow (timing and control), Effort (muscle action patterns), Respiration (breathing rhythms and reach). Used to diagnose movement inefficiency without forcing correction - awareness precedes change.

Somatic education system developed by Moshe Feldenkrais. Core principle: awareness through movement, not correction through force. Constraint-based - reduce options to reveal efficient patterns. Directly influenced llama-conductor design philosophy: structural constraints over reward shaping, fail-loud over silent degradation. 'If you know what you're doing, you can do what you want.'

BJJ learning methodology emphasising real combat scenarios over rote technique drilling. Three principles: (1) learning through experience in realistic situations, (2) adaptability and problem-solving under live resistance, (3) task-focused games and sparring that mimic competition. Opposes step-by-step isolation drilling. Parallel in software: test against real conditions, not synthetic fixtures.

BJJ principle: control position before attempting submission. Mount > back > side control > guard > bottom. Attempting submissions from poor position = low percentage, high risk. Applied to system design: establish stable state before adding features. Don't attempt complex operations from unstable architecture.

Defensive/offensive BJJ position where the bottom player uses legs to control, sweep, or submit the top player. Closed guard (legs locked), open guard (legs framing), half guard (one leg trapped). Metaphor Bobby uses: guard = defensive constraint layer that still enables offensive options. Harness design equivalent: fail-loud guards that don't block forward progress.

Submission signal in BJJ - acknowledge the technique worked, reset, learn. No shame in tapping to a good technique. Applied to engineering: when a design choice is clearly wrong, tap early and reset rather than defending a bad position. Ego defence of bad architecture is the equivalent of not tapping - you just get hurt more.

Live sparring in BJJ. The real test - not drilling, not theory, not demonstration. Everything gets stress-tested against a resisting partner. Bobby's engineering equivalent: running the actual probe battery against live router, not synthetic fixtures. 'Rolling' is what reveals what actually works.

Learning methodology used in both Feldenkrais and ecological BJJ: reduce degrees of freedom to reveal efficient patterns, then gradually release constraints as competence grows. Directly maps to llama-conductor design - structural constraints on model behavior produce reliable outputs without retraining. The constraint IS the instruction.

In rehabilitation and motor learning: variability in movement patterns is a feature, not a bug. Rigid repetition produces fragile skill; variable practice under changing conditions produces robust skill. Applied to swarm design: worker diversity (three model families) is intentional - correlated errors are the real risk, not variability.

Rehabilitation principle: stable core/proximal segments enable efficient distal movement. You can't throw a punch from an unstable base. Engineering equivalent: stable session state and deterministic routing (proximal) enable complex generation and retrieval (distal). Fix the base before optimising the edges.

In motor control: the number of independent variables in a movement system. Too many degrees of freedom = coordination problem (Bernstein's problem). Solution: freeze degrees of freedom initially, release as skill develops. Bobby's design instinct: constrain first, release later. Never start with full freedom and try to add constraints retrospectively.

The sense of body position and movement in space - the 'hidden sense'. In BJJ: knowing where your limbs are without looking, feeling leverage and weight shifts. In system design: internal telemetry that tells the system where it is without external observation. KAIOKEN turn classifier is proprioceptive - reads internal signals to orient routing.

Short-form reference to the Feldenkrais Method. Core principle: awareness through movement, not correction through force. Constraint-based — reduce options to reveal efficient patterns. If you know what you are doing, you can do what you want. See: Feldenkrais Method entry for full detail.
